<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]

{{Short description|Japanese translation of a Chinese term for zazen}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{Zen Buddhism}}
{{nihongo|'''''Shikantaza'''''|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ}} is [[:en:Dogen|Dogen]]'s {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} translation of the {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}),{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "focus on meditative practice alone", although many modern Western practitioners have interpreted this very differently.{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} The phrase was used by Dogen's teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}}, a monk of the [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan|1=Chan Buddhism}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, to refer to the {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=meditation|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}-practice called "silent illumination" ({{zh|s=й»ҳз…§зҰ…}}), or "serene reflection", taught by the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ“1157).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} In Japan, it is associated with the {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} [[:en:Soto school|Soto school]], Dogen's offshoot of Caodong. Some practitioners teach that ''shikantaza'' means that one should not focus attention on a specific object (such as the breath), instead "just sitting" in a state of conscious awareness; however, the 13th-century origin of the expression indicates a general emphasis on meditation in any form as sufficient for spiritual enlightenment. The original teaching was meant to criticize the complicated ceremony, abstruse study, endless tracing of spiritual lineage, and other aspects of Buddhism that even by the 12th century had been identified as excessive.

==Etymology and meaning==
The term ''shikantaza'' is the [[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|Sino-Japanese reading]]{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} of ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' (еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ){{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just sitting",{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} "nothing but sitting",{{sfnp|Fischer-Schreiber|Schuhmacher|Woerner|1989|p=321}} "meditation of just sitting",<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just mind [yourself] sitting".{{sfnp|Shengyen|2008|p=94}} ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}) translates as follows:
* ''zhЗҗguЗҺn'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}}, J. ''shikan''; or {{lang|zh|зҘҮз®Ў}}, with {{lang|zh|зҘҮ}} [zhЗҗ] serving as a variant of {{lang|zh|еҸӘ}} [zhЗҗ]<ref group=web name="Muller"/>), "by all means; merely, simply; only concerned with",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to focus exclusively on";<ref group=web name="Muller"/>
* ''dЗҺzuГІ'' {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}}, "[Buddhism/Daoism] sit in meditation",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to {{ill|Squatting position|en|Squatting position|WD=Q2540134|lt=squat|1=Squatting position}}, sit down cross-legged", which corresponds with {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} ''utkuб№ӯuka-stha'';<ref group=web name="Muller"/>

The inspiration for this teaching derives from a pivotal episode reportedly occurring sometime in the early 1220s (Song dynasty), at {{ill|Tiantong Temple|en|Tiantong Temple|WD=Q10940602|lt=Tiantong Mountain Monastery|1=Tiantong Temple}} ({{lang|zh|еӨ©з«ҘеҜә}}, also known as Jingde Monastery {{lang|zh|жҷҜеҫ·еҜә}}, east of modern-day {{ill|Ningbo|en|Ningbo|WD=Q42780|lt=Ningbo|1=Ningbo}}). An exchange took place between the eminent Chinese {{ill|Caodong school|en|Caodong school|WD=Q1964867|lt=Caodong|1=Caodong school}} teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}} and his disciples. In particular, it focuses on an inspiration by one of Rujing's Japanese disciples, {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}, who would later found the [[SЕҚtЕҚ|SЕҚtЕҚ Zen]] sect:

{{Blockquote|text=Then, one day during late night seated meditation, Reverend Jing entered the hall and admonished the great assembly for sleeping, saying:

"Inquiring into Zen is the sloughing off of body and mind [{{lang|zh|иә«еҝғи„ұиҗҪ}}].
''There is no need for burning incense, making prostrations, recollecting buddhas, practicing repentances, or reading sЕ«tras.'' Just sit [in meditation] [{{lang|zh|зҘҮз®ЎгҒ«жү“еқҗ}}]; only then will you attain it."

At that time, hearing this, the Master [DЕҚgen] immediately had a great awakening... .<ref>{{cite book |last1=Foulk |first1=T. Griffith |title=DenkЕҚroku (Record of the Transmission) of Illumination by the Great Ancestor, Zen Master Keizan, Vol. II |date=2017вҖ“2018 |publisher=SЕҚtЕҚshЕ« ShЕ«muchЕҚ |page=535-536 |url=https://www.sotozen.com/eng/library/denkoroku/index.html |access-date=28 July 2024 |chapter=51}}</ref> [emphasis added]}}

While T.G. Foulk's translation here reads only "sit", he and other interpreters clarify that the meaning of {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}} is generally broad, meaning more than simply sitting.{{sfnp|Buswell Jr.|Lopez Jr.|2014|p=Entry: shikan taza}} The original exchange between Rujing and his disciples indicates a clear meaning of the teaching: that high-flung ceremony and study are unnecessary and irrelevant, that {{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|WD=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}}, {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=dhyana|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}, and similar meditation practice of whatever kind (whether sitting, resting, breathing, gazing at a scene, walking, or simply engaging in silence) should be sufficiently effective.

{{ill|James Ishmael Ford|en|James Ishmael Ford|WD=Q6136610|lt=James Ishmael Ford|1=James Ishmael Ford}} states that "some trace the root of this word [''shikantaza''] to the Japanese pronunciation of Sanskrit [[:en:Vipassana|Vipassana]], though this is far from certain."{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} This etymological error about {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} (''shikan'', "only", "just") is rooted in the fact that Japanese has many [[:en:homophones|homophones]] pronounced ''shikan''. It stems from a more commonly used Japanese word, namely {{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}} (''shikan'', "concentration and observation"{{refn|group=note|name=TSS|Watanabe ToshirЕҚ (жёЎйӮҠж•ҸйғҺ), Edmund R. Skrzypczak, and Paul Snowden, eds. (2003), ''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'' (ж–°е’ҢиӢұеӨ§иҫһе…ё), 5th edition, Kenkyusha, 1125. This bilingual dictionary lists жӯўиҰі and 21 other words pronounced ''shikan'' (e.g., д»•е®ҳ "government service" and ејӣз·© "relaxation") but not ''shikan'' еҸӘз®Ў.}}) (as practiced by the Tendai sect) that translates the Sanskrit "''{{ill|Еҡamatha|en|Еҡamatha|WD=Q1569252|lt=Еҡamatha|1=Еҡamatha}}'' and ''[[:en:VipaЕӣyanДҒ|VipaЕӣyanДҒ]]''," the two basic forms of {{ill|Buddhist meditation|en|Buddhist meditation|WD=Q1207203|lt=Buddhist meditation|1=Buddhist meditation}}.{{refn|group=note|The term ''shikan'' ({{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}}) is derived from Chinese ''zhЗҗguДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|жӯўи§Җ}}, "[Buddhism] keep mental calm while observing the universe" (cf. the ''[[Mohe Zhiguan]]''){{harvtxt|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267}}), which [[compound (linguistics)|compounds]] ''shi'' or ''zhЗҗ'' ({{lang|zh|жӯў}} ("stop", "stabilize", "''Еӣamatha''") and ''kan'' or ''guДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|и§Җ}}, "observe", "contemplate", "''vipaЕӣyanДҒ''"). An instance of the confusion of {{lang|zh|жӯўиҰі}} for {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} is [[Steve Hagen]]'s claim that "''shi'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|еҸӘ}}'] means tranquility [= '{{lang|zh|жӯў}}'], ''kan'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|з®Ў}}'] means awareness [= '{{lang|zh|иҰі}}'], ''ta'' means hitting exactly the right spot (not one atom off), and ''za'' means to sit."{{harvtxt|Hagen|2007|p=189}}}}

The phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ("just sitting") was used by {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}'s teacher [[:en:Tiantong Rujing|Tiantong Rujing]] (1162вҖ“1228) for silent illumination{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} (Chinese ''mГІzhГ o'' й»ҳз…§; Japanese {{Transliteration|ja|mokushЕҚ}}).{{sfnp|Benson|1989}} According to Koten Benson, in ''mochao'' 
{{quote|The first character, mo, has an element in it that means black or darkness, making the whole character signify "dark, secret, silent, serene, profound" and also "to close the lips, to become silent". The second character, chao, has as element meaning "the brightness of the sun". The whole character translates as "to reflect light, to shine on, to illume or enlighten", as well as "to reflect upon, to look upon, to have insight into". The whole term thus becomes "serene reflection", "silent illumination" or "luminescent darkness".{{sfnp|Benson|1989}}}}

==Practice==
===Classical sources===
"Silent illumination" or "silent reflection" was the hallmark of the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese|1=China}} [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of [[:en:Chinese ChГЎn|Chan]].<ref group="web">Muller, A. Charles, ed.: The {{ill|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|en|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|WD=Q16961807|lt=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|1=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism}}, ed. of 04/03/2008, Chinese Readings Index (Pinyin System) [http://buddhism-dict.net/ddb/indexes/read-zh-py.html]</ref> The first Chan teacher to articulate silent illumination was the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ”1157), who wrote an inscription entitled "silent illumination meditation" (''MokushЕҚ zen'' й»ҳз…§зҰ… or ''MГІzhГ o chГЎn'' й»ҳз…§зҰӘ).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=xii}} Sheng-yen explains that

{{blockquote|In silent illumination, "just sitting" is only the first step. While you maintain the sitting posture, you should also try to establish the "silent" state of the mind. Eventually you reach a point where the mind does not move and yet is very clear. That unmoving mind is "silent," and that clarity of mind is "illumination." This is the meaning of "silent illumination."<ref>Sheng-yen (lecture, 1993), [https://www.ddmbachicago.org/chan/methods/silentillumination/ ''The Silent Illumination Method'']</ref>}}

With the phrase ''shikantaza'' DЕҚgen means "doing only zazen whole-heartedly"{{sfnp|Akishige|1977|p=18}} or "single-minded sitting."{{sfnp|Shaner|1985|p=158}}  According to Merv Fowler, shikantaza is described best as "quiet sitting in open awareness, reflecting directly the reality of life."{{sfnp|Fowler|2005|p=96}} According to Austin, ''shikantaza'' is "an alert condition, performed erect, with no trace of sluggishness or drowsiness."{{sfnp|Austin|1998|p=76}} Fred Reinhard Dallmayr writes, 
{{quote|Regarding practice, Dogen counseled a distinctly nonattached or nonclinging kind of action, that is, an activity completely unconcerned with benefits or the accomplishment of ulterior goals: the activity of 'just sitting' or 'nothing-but-sitting' (''shikantaza'') whereby self-seeking is set aside in a manner resembling a resolute 'dropping off of body and mind.'{{sfnp|Dallmayr|1996|p=178-179}}}}

===Modern sources===
Zen master {{ill|John Daido Loori|en|John Daido Loori|WD=Q6228392|lt=John Daido Loori|1=John Daido Loori}} describes ''shikantaza'' as a challenging practice in spite of its name's simplicity. Mental strength (''joriki'') is not achieved through sustained concentration as in {{ill|Anapanasati|en|Anapanasati|WD=Q2123673|lt=breath meditation|1=Anapanasati}}, but through awareness of the flow of mind, without actively attempting to let go of a thought.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} The user must watch its thoughts, "without analyzing them, judging them, attempting to understand or categorize them," being only aware of them.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} According to him, this helps mental activity move on and produce {{ill|Samadhi|en|Samadhi|WD=Q2217023|lt=Samadhi|1=Samadhi}}.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}}

{{quote|When you're doing ''shikantaza'' you don't try to focus on anything specifically, or to make thoughts go away. You simply allow everything to be just the way it is. Thoughts come, thoughts go, and you simply watch them, you keep your awareness on them. It takes a lot of energy and persistence to sit ''shikantaza'', to not get caught up in daydreaming. But little by little, thoughts begin to slow down, and finally they cease to arise. When the thought disappears, the thinker disappears.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}}}

Commenting on Loori's words, meditation expert Eric Harrison likens ''shikantaza'' to a {{ill|Psychology|en|Psychology|WD=Q9418|lt=psychological|1=Psychology}} process of {{ill|Extinction (psychology)|en|Extinction (psychology)|WD=Q1385098|lt=extinction|1=Extinction (psychology)}}, in which repeated reduction of a behavioral response eventually leads to no response.{{sfnp|Harrison|2017}}

Loori describes awareness as the one thing necessary to the practice of shikantaza.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} This requires a heightened state of mental alertness, which he warns cannot be maintained for too long periods of time. He recommends to practice ''shikantaza'' half an hour to an hour, then stand up and practice ''{{ill|Walking meditation|en|Walking meditation|WD=Q571062|lt=kinhin|1=Walking meditation}}'' in order to relax the mind before sitting down and continuing.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}

{{ill|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|en|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|WD=Q572599|lt=ShunryЕ« Suzuki|1=ShunryЕ« Suzuki}} states about ''shikantaza'', "do not try to stop your mind, but leave everything as it is. Then things will not stay in your mind for so long. Things will come as they come and go as they go. Eventually your clear, empty mind will last fairly long."{{sfnp|Davis|2022|p=293}} For his part, describing the practice's goal as being simply aware of thoughts without getting caught by them, Sean Murphy cites {{ill|Taizan Maezumi|en|Taizan Maezumi|WD=Q1952144|lt=Taizan Maezumi|1=Taizan Maezumi}} as advising to "regard our thoughts as if they were clouds, watching them as they drift from one end of the mind to the other, but making no attempt to hold onto them - and when they pass over the horizon, as they inevitably will, making no attempt to grasp after them.<ref>Sean Murphy, "Guided Meditation: Passing Thoughts", ''{{ill|Yoga Journal|en|Yoga Journal|WD=Q1027421|lt=Yoga Journal|1=Yoga Journal}}'', JanuaryвҖ“February 2003</ref>

Jundo Cohen warns that its meaning of "just sitting" must not be taken too literally, and underlines the importance of awareness.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} When faced against strong emotions or anxious thoughts, Cohen instructs to simply observe them with {{ill|Upekб№ЈДҒ|en|Upekб№ЈДҒ|WD=Q2165710|lt=equanimity|1=Upekб№ЈДҒ}}, "treating them like passing weather clouds". At the same time, he stresses not to play with and being pulled in by thoughts.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} He compares ''shikantaza'' to "the children's puzzle of {{ill|Chinese finger trap|en|Chinese finger trap|WD=Q1384990|lt=Chinese finger cuffs|1=Chinese finger trap}}, which are escaped not by forceful effort, but by non-resistance". Only by dropping the hunt for {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}, accepting everything without grasping or avoiding, can enlightenment be found in it.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}}

====Similar techniques====
A modern technique described as similar to ''shikantaza'' is called "Do Nothing Meditation" by {{ill|Shinzen Young|en|Shinzen Young|WD=Q7497849|lt=Shinzen Young|1=Shinzen Young}}. The user is instructed to let go of all mental intentions, without trying to meditate or concentrate in any way. Any distraction or thought is allowed, unless the user feels they are intentionally thinking or doing something, in whose case they must stop this intention and let it go, including any possible struggle at it. As a result, "eventually the mind feels very spacious, open, and relaxed, but also bright, clear, and vivid".<ref>{{cite web|last=Taft|first=Michael W.|url=https://deconstructingyourself.com/do-nothing-meditation.html|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=September 11, 2017|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Deconstructingyourself.com}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.anahana.com/en/wellbeing-blog/do-nothing-meditation|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=August 25, 2022|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Anahana}}</ref>

Another similar description comes from [[:en:Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj|Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj]], in ''{{ill|I Am That|en|I Am That|WD=Q5976093|lt=I Am That|1=I Am That}}'', where he recommends "letting thoughts flow and watching them and to keep the mind quiet. "The state of freedom from all thoughts will happen suddenly and by the bliss of it you shall recognize it."<ref>Nisargadatta Maharaj, ''I Am That'', p.224-225</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]

{{Short description|Japanese translation of a Chinese term for zazen}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{Zen Buddhism}}
{{nihongo|'''''Shikantaza'''''|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ}} is [[:en:Dogen|Dogen]]'s {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} translation of the {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}),{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "focus on meditative practice alone", although many modern Western practitioners have interpreted this very differently.{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} The phrase was used by Dogen's teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}}, a monk of the [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan|1=Chan Buddhism}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, to refer to the {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=meditation|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}-practice called "silent illumination" ({{zh|s=й»ҳз…§зҰ…}}), or "serene reflection", taught by the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ“1157).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} In Japan, it is associated with the {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} [[:en:Soto school|Soto school]], Dogen's offshoot of Caodong. Some practitioners teach that ''shikantaza'' means that one should not focus attention on a specific object (such as the breath), instead "just sitting" in a state of conscious awareness; however, the 13th-century origin of the expression indicates a general emphasis on meditation in any form as sufficient for spiritual enlightenment. The original teaching was meant to criticize the complicated ceremony, abstruse study, endless tracing of spiritual lineage, and other aspects of Buddhism that even by the 12th century had been identified as excessive.

==Etymology and meaning==
The term ''shikantaza'' is the [[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|Sino-Japanese reading]]{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} of ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' (еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ){{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just sitting",{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} "nothing but sitting",{{sfnp|Fischer-Schreiber|Schuhmacher|Woerner|1989|p=321}} "meditation of just sitting",<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just mind [yourself] sitting".{{sfnp|Shengyen|2008|p=94}} ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}) translates as follows:
* ''zhЗҗguЗҺn'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}}, J. ''shikan''; or {{lang|zh|зҘҮз®Ў}}, with {{lang|zh|зҘҮ}} [zhЗҗ] serving as a variant of {{lang|zh|еҸӘ}} [zhЗҗ]<ref group=web name="Muller"/>), "by all means; merely, simply; only concerned with",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to focus exclusively on";<ref group=web name="Muller"/>
* ''dЗҺzuГІ'' {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}}, "[Buddhism/Daoism] sit in meditation",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to {{ill|Squatting position|en|Squatting position|WD=Q2540134|lt=squat|1=Squatting position}}, sit down cross-legged", which corresponds with {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} ''utkuб№ӯuka-stha'';<ref group=web name="Muller"/>

The inspiration for this teaching derives from a pivotal episode reportedly occurring sometime in the early 1220s (Song dynasty), at {{ill|Tiantong Temple|en|Tiantong Temple|WD=Q10940602|lt=Tiantong Mountain Monastery|1=Tiantong Temple}} ({{lang|zh|еӨ©з«ҘеҜә}}, also known as Jingde Monastery {{lang|zh|жҷҜеҫ·еҜә}}, east of modern-day {{ill|Ningbo|en|Ningbo|WD=Q42780|lt=Ningbo|1=Ningbo}}). An exchange took place between the eminent Chinese {{ill|Caodong school|en|Caodong school|WD=Q1964867|lt=Caodong|1=Caodong school}} teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}} and his disciples. In particular, it focuses on an inspiration by one of Rujing's Japanese disciples, {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}, who would later found the [[SЕҚtЕҚ|SЕҚtЕҚ Zen]] sect:

{{Blockquote|text=Then, one day during late night seated meditation, Reverend Jing entered the hall and admonished the great assembly for sleeping, saying:

"Inquiring into Zen is the sloughing off of body and mind [{{lang|zh|иә«еҝғи„ұиҗҪ}}].
''There is no need for burning incense, making prostrations, recollecting buddhas, practicing repentances, or reading sЕ«tras.'' Just sit [in meditation] [{{lang|zh|зҘҮз®ЎгҒ«жү“еқҗ}}]; only then will you attain it."

At that time, hearing this, the Master [DЕҚgen] immediately had a great awakening... .<ref>{{cite book |last1=Foulk |first1=T. Griffith |title=DenkЕҚroku (Record of the Transmission) of Illumination by the Great Ancestor, Zen Master Keizan, Vol. II |date=2017вҖ“2018 |publisher=SЕҚtЕҚshЕ« ShЕ«muchЕҚ |page=535-536 |url=https://www.sotozen.com/eng/library/denkoroku/index.html |access-date=28 July 2024 |chapter=51}}</ref> [emphasis added]}}

While T.G. Foulk's translation here reads only "sit", he and other interpreters clarify that the meaning of {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}} is generally broad, meaning more than simply sitting.{{sfnp|Buswell Jr.|Lopez Jr.|2014|p=Entry: shikan taza}} The original exchange between Rujing and his disciples indicates a clear meaning of the teaching: that high-flung ceremony and study are unnecessary and irrelevant, that {{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|WD=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}}, {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=dhyana|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}, and similar meditation practice of whatever kind (whether sitting, resting, breathing, gazing at a scene, walking, or simply engaging in silence) should be sufficiently effective.

{{ill|James Ishmael Ford|en|James Ishmael Ford|WD=Q6136610|lt=James Ishmael Ford|1=James Ishmael Ford}} states that "some trace the root of this word [''shikantaza''] to the Japanese pronunciation of Sanskrit [[:en:Vipassana|Vipassana]], though this is far from certain."{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} This etymological error about {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} (''shikan'', "only", "just") is rooted in the fact that Japanese has many [[:en:homophones|homophones]] pronounced ''shikan''. It stems from a more commonly used Japanese word, namely {{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}} (''shikan'', "concentration and observation"{{refn|group=note|name=TSS|Watanabe ToshirЕҚ (жёЎйӮҠж•ҸйғҺ), Edmund R. Skrzypczak, and Paul Snowden, eds. (2003), ''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'' (ж–°е’ҢиӢұеӨ§иҫһе…ё), 5th edition, Kenkyusha, 1125. This bilingual dictionary lists жӯўиҰі and 21 other words pronounced ''shikan'' (e.g., д»•е®ҳ "government service" and ејӣз·© "relaxation") but not ''shikan'' еҸӘз®Ў.}}) (as practiced by the Tendai sect) that translates the Sanskrit "''{{ill|Еҡamatha|en|Еҡamatha|WD=Q1569252|lt=Еҡamatha|1=Еҡamatha}}'' and ''[[:en:VipaЕӣyanДҒ|VipaЕӣyanДҒ]]''," the two basic forms of {{ill|Buddhist meditation|en|Buddhist meditation|WD=Q1207203|lt=Buddhist meditation|1=Buddhist meditation}}.{{refn|group=note|The term ''shikan'' ({{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}}) is derived from Chinese ''zhЗҗguДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|жӯўи§Җ}}, "[Buddhism] keep mental calm while observing the universe" (cf. the ''[[Mohe Zhiguan]]''){{harvtxt|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267}}), which [[compound (linguistics)|compounds]] ''shi'' or ''zhЗҗ'' ({{lang|zh|жӯў}} ("stop", "stabilize", "''Еӣamatha''") and ''kan'' or ''guДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|и§Җ}}, "observe", "contemplate", "''vipaЕӣyanДҒ''"). An instance of the confusion of {{lang|zh|жӯўиҰі}} for {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} is [[Steve Hagen]]'s claim that "''shi'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|еҸӘ}}'] means tranquility [= '{{lang|zh|жӯў}}'], ''kan'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|з®Ў}}'] means awareness [= '{{lang|zh|иҰі}}'], ''ta'' means hitting exactly the right spot (not one atom off), and ''za'' means to sit."{{harvtxt|Hagen|2007|p=189}}}}

The phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ("just sitting") was used by {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}'s teacher [[:en:Tiantong Rujing|Tiantong Rujing]] (1162вҖ“1228) for silent illumination{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} (Chinese ''mГІzhГ o'' й»ҳз…§; Japanese {{Transliteration|ja|mokushЕҚ}}).{{sfnp|Benson|1989}} According to Koten Benson, in ''mochao'' 
{{quote|The first character, mo, has an element in it that means black or darkness, making the whole character signify "dark, secret, silent, serene, profound" and also "to close the lips, to become silent". The second character, chao, has as element meaning "the brightness of the sun". The whole character translates as "to reflect light, to shine on, to illume or enlighten", as well as "to reflect upon, to look upon, to have insight into". The whole term thus becomes "serene reflection", "silent illumination" or "luminescent darkness".{{sfnp|Benson|1989}}}}

==Practice==
===Classical sources===
"Silent illumination" or "silent reflection" was the hallmark of the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese|1=China}} [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of [[:en:Chinese ChГЎn|Chan]].<ref group="web">Muller, A. Charles, ed.: The {{ill|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|en|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|WD=Q16961807|lt=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|1=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism}}, ed. of 04/03/2008, Chinese Readings Index (Pinyin System) [http://buddhism-dict.net/ddb/indexes/read-zh-py.html]</ref> The first Chan teacher to articulate silent illumination was the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ”1157), who wrote an inscription entitled "silent illumination meditation" (''MokushЕҚ zen'' й»ҳз…§зҰ… or ''MГІzhГ o chГЎn'' й»ҳз…§зҰӘ).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=xii}} Sheng-yen explains that

{{blockquote|In silent illumination, "just sitting" is only the first step. While you maintain the sitting posture, you should also try to establish the "silent" state of the mind. Eventually you reach a point where the mind does not move and yet is very clear. That unmoving mind is "silent," and that clarity of mind is "illumination." This is the meaning of "silent illumination."<ref>Sheng-yen (lecture, 1993), [https://www.ddmbachicago.org/chan/methods/silentillumination/ ''The Silent Illumination Method'']</ref>}}

With the phrase ''shikantaza'' DЕҚgen means "doing only zazen whole-heartedly"{{sfnp|Akishige|1977|p=18}} or "single-minded sitting."{{sfnp|Shaner|1985|p=158}}  According to Merv Fowler, shikantaza is described best as "quiet sitting in open awareness, reflecting directly the reality of life."{{sfnp|Fowler|2005|p=96}} According to Austin, ''shikantaza'' is "an alert condition, performed erect, with no trace of sluggishness or drowsiness."{{sfnp|Austin|1998|p=76}} Fred Reinhard Dallmayr writes, 
{{quote|Regarding practice, Dogen counseled a distinctly nonattached or nonclinging kind of action, that is, an activity completely unconcerned with benefits or the accomplishment of ulterior goals: the activity of 'just sitting' or 'nothing-but-sitting' (''shikantaza'') whereby self-seeking is set aside in a manner resembling a resolute 'dropping off of body and mind.'{{sfnp|Dallmayr|1996|p=178-179}}}}

===Modern sources===
Zen master {{ill|John Daido Loori|en|John Daido Loori|WD=Q6228392|lt=John Daido Loori|1=John Daido Loori}} describes ''shikantaza'' as a challenging practice in spite of its name's simplicity. Mental strength (''joriki'') is not achieved through sustained concentration as in {{ill|Anapanasati|en|Anapanasati|WD=Q2123673|lt=breath meditation|1=Anapanasati}}, but through awareness of the flow of mind, without actively attempting to let go of a thought.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} The user must watch its thoughts, "without analyzing them, judging them, attempting to understand or categorize them," being only aware of them.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} According to him, this helps mental activity move on and produce {{ill|Samadhi|en|Samadhi|WD=Q2217023|lt=Samadhi|1=Samadhi}}.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}}

{{quote|When you're doing ''shikantaza'' you don't try to focus on anything specifically, or to make thoughts go away. You simply allow everything to be just the way it is. Thoughts come, thoughts go, and you simply watch them, you keep your awareness on them. It takes a lot of energy and persistence to sit ''shikantaza'', to not get caught up in daydreaming. But little by little, thoughts begin to slow down, and finally they cease to arise. When the thought disappears, the thinker disappears.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}}}

Commenting on Loori's words, meditation expert Eric Harrison likens ''shikantaza'' to a {{ill|Psychology|en|Psychology|WD=Q9418|lt=psychological|1=Psychology}} process of {{ill|Extinction (psychology)|en|Extinction (psychology)|WD=Q1385098|lt=extinction|1=Extinction (psychology)}}, in which repeated reduction of a behavioral response eventually leads to no response.{{sfnp|Harrison|2017}}

Loori describes awareness as the one thing necessary to the practice of shikantaza.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} This requires a heightened state of mental alertness, which he warns cannot be maintained for too long periods of time. He recommends to practice ''shikantaza'' half an hour to an hour, then stand up and practice ''{{ill|Walking meditation|en|Walking meditation|WD=Q571062|lt=kinhin|1=Walking meditation}}'' in order to relax the mind before sitting down and continuing.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}

{{ill|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|en|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|WD=Q572599|lt=ShunryЕ« Suzuki|1=ShunryЕ« Suzuki}} states about ''shikantaza'', "do not try to stop your mind, but leave everything as it is. Then things will not stay in your mind for so long. Things will come as they come and go as they go. Eventually your clear, empty mind will last fairly long."{{sfnp|Davis|2022|p=293}} For his part, describing the practice's goal as being simply aware of thoughts without getting caught by them, Sean Murphy cites {{ill|Taizan Maezumi|en|Taizan Maezumi|WD=Q1952144|lt=Taizan Maezumi|1=Taizan Maezumi}} as advising to "regard our thoughts as if they were clouds, watching them as they drift from one end of the mind to the other, but making no attempt to hold onto them - and when they pass over the horizon, as they inevitably will, making no attempt to grasp after them.<ref>Sean Murphy, "Guided Meditation: Passing Thoughts", ''{{ill|Yoga Journal|en|Yoga Journal|WD=Q1027421|lt=Yoga Journal|1=Yoga Journal}}'', JanuaryвҖ“February 2003</ref>

Jundo Cohen warns that its meaning of "just sitting" must not be taken too literally, and underlines the importance of awareness.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} When faced against strong emotions or anxious thoughts, Cohen instructs to simply observe them with {{ill|Upekб№ЈДҒ|en|Upekб№ЈДҒ|WD=Q2165710|lt=equanimity|1=Upekб№ЈДҒ}}, "treating them like passing weather clouds". At the same time, he stresses not to play with and being pulled in by thoughts.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} He compares ''shikantaza'' to "the children's puzzle of {{ill|Chinese finger trap|en|Chinese finger trap|WD=Q1384990|lt=Chinese finger cuffs|1=Chinese finger trap}}, which are escaped not by forceful effort, but by non-resistance". Only by dropping the hunt for {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}, accepting everything without grasping or avoiding, can enlightenment be found in it.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}}

====Similar techniques====
A modern technique described as similar to ''shikantaza'' is called "Do Nothing Meditation" by {{ill|Shinzen Young|en|Shinzen Young|WD=Q7497849|lt=Shinzen Young|1=Shinzen Young}}. The user is instructed to let go of all mental intentions, without trying to meditate or concentrate in any way. Any distraction or thought is allowed, unless the user feels they are intentionally thinking or doing something, in whose case they must stop this intention and let it go, including any possible struggle at it. As a result, "eventually the mind feels very spacious, open, and relaxed, but also bright, clear, and vivid".<ref>{{cite web|last=Taft|first=Michael W.|url=https://deconstructingyourself.com/do-nothing-meditation.html|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=September 11, 2017|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Deconstructingyourself.com}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.anahana.com/en/wellbeing-blog/do-nothing-meditation|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=August 25, 2022|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Anahana}}</ref>

Another similar description comes from [[:en:Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj|Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj]], in ''{{ill|I Am That|en|I Am That|WD=Q5976093|lt=I Am That|1=I Am That}}'', where he recommends "letting thoughts flow and watching them and to keep the mind quiet. "The state of freedom from all thoughts will happen suddenly and by the bliss of it you shall recognize it."<ref>Nisargadatta Maharaj, ''I Am That'', p.224-225</ref>

==See also==

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]

{{Short description|Japanese translation of a Chinese term for zazen}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{Zen Buddhism}}
{{nihongo|'''''Shikantaza'''''|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ}} is [[:en:Dogen|Dogen]]'s {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} translation of the {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}),{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "focus on meditative practice alone", although many modern Western practitioners have interpreted this very differently.{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} The phrase was used by Dogen's teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}}, a monk of the [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan|1=Chan Buddhism}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, to refer to the {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=meditation|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}-practice called "silent illumination" ({{zh|s=й»ҳз…§зҰ…}}), or "serene reflection", taught by the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ“1157).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} In Japan, it is associated with the {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} [[:en:Soto school|Soto school]], Dogen's offshoot of Caodong. Some practitioners teach that ''shikantaza'' means that one should not focus attention on a specific object (such as the breath), instead "just sitting" in a state of conscious awareness; however, the 13th-century origin of the expression indicates a general emphasis on meditation in any form as sufficient for spiritual enlightenment. The original teaching was meant to criticize the complicated ceremony, abstruse study, endless tracing of spiritual lineage, and other aspects of Buddhism that even by the 12th century had been identified as excessive.

==Etymology and meaning==
The term ''shikantaza'' is the [[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|Sino-Japanese reading]]{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} of ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' (еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ){{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just sitting",{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} "nothing but sitting",{{sfnp|Fischer-Schreiber|Schuhmacher|Woerner|1989|p=321}} "meditation of just sitting",<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just mind [yourself] sitting".{{sfnp|Shengyen|2008|p=94}} ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}) translates as follows:
* ''zhЗҗguЗҺn'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}}, J. ''shikan''; or {{lang|zh|зҘҮз®Ў}}, with {{lang|zh|зҘҮ}} [zhЗҗ] serving as a variant of {{lang|zh|еҸӘ}} [zhЗҗ]<ref group=web name="Muller"/>), "by all means; merely, simply; only concerned with",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to focus exclusively on";<ref group=web name="Muller"/>
* ''dЗҺzuГІ'' {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}}, "[Buddhism/Daoism] sit in meditation",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to {{ill|Squatting position|en|Squatting position|WD=Q2540134|lt=squat|1=Squatting position}}, sit down cross-legged", which corresponds with {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} ''utkuб№ӯuka-stha'';<ref group=web name="Muller"/>

The inspiration for this teaching derives from a pivotal episode reportedly occurring sometime in the early 1220s (Song dynasty), at {{ill|Tiantong Temple|en|Tiantong Temple|WD=Q10940602|lt=Tiantong Mountain Monastery|1=Tiantong Temple}} ({{lang|zh|еӨ©з«ҘеҜә}}, also known as Jingde Monastery {{lang|zh|жҷҜеҫ·еҜә}}, east of modern-day {{ill|Ningbo|en|Ningbo|WD=Q42780|lt=Ningbo|1=Ningbo}}). An exchange took place between the eminent Chinese {{ill|Caodong school|en|Caodong school|WD=Q1964867|lt=Caodong|1=Caodong school}} teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}} and his disciples. In particular, it focuses on an inspiration by one of Rujing's Japanese disciples, {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}, who would later found the [[SЕҚtЕҚ|SЕҚtЕҚ Zen]] sect:

{{Blockquote|text=Then, one day during late night seated meditation, Reverend Jing entered the hall and admonished the great assembly for sleeping, saying:

"Inquiring into Zen is the sloughing off of body and mind [{{lang|zh|иә«еҝғи„ұиҗҪ}}].
''There is no need for burning incense, making prostrations, recollecting buddhas, practicing repentances, or reading sЕ«tras.'' Just sit [in meditation] [{{lang|zh|зҘҮз®ЎгҒ«жү“еқҗ}}]; only then will you attain it."

At that time, hearing this, the Master [DЕҚgen] immediately had a great awakening... .<ref>{{cite book |last1=Foulk |first1=T. Griffith |title=DenkЕҚroku (Record of the Transmission) of Illumination by the Great Ancestor, Zen Master Keizan, Vol. II |date=2017вҖ“2018 |publisher=SЕҚtЕҚshЕ« ShЕ«muchЕҚ |page=535-536 |url=https://www.sotozen.com/eng/library/denkoroku/index.html |access-date=28 July 2024 |chapter=51}}</ref> [emphasis added]}}

While T.G. Foulk's translation here reads only "sit", he and other interpreters clarify that the meaning of {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}} is generally broad, meaning more than simply sitting.{{sfnp|Buswell Jr.|Lopez Jr.|2014|p=Entry: shikan taza}} The original exchange between Rujing and his disciples indicates a clear meaning of the teaching: that high-flung ceremony and study are unnecessary and irrelevant, that {{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|WD=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}}, {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=dhyana|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}, and similar meditation practice of whatever kind (whether sitting, resting, breathing, gazing at a scene, walking, or simply engaging in silence) should be sufficiently effective.

{{ill|James Ishmael Ford|en|James Ishmael Ford|WD=Q6136610|lt=James Ishmael Ford|1=James Ishmael Ford}} states that "some trace the root of this word [''shikantaza''] to the Japanese pronunciation of Sanskrit [[:en:Vipassana|Vipassana]], though this is far from certain."{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} This etymological error about {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} (''shikan'', "only", "just") is rooted in the fact that Japanese has many [[:en:homophones|homophones]] pronounced ''shikan''. It stems from a more commonly used Japanese word, namely {{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}} (''shikan'', "concentration and observation"{{refn|group=note|name=TSS|Watanabe ToshirЕҚ (жёЎйӮҠж•ҸйғҺ), Edmund R. Skrzypczak, and Paul Snowden, eds. (2003), ''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'' (ж–°е’ҢиӢұеӨ§иҫһе…ё), 5th edition, Kenkyusha, 1125. This bilingual dictionary lists жӯўиҰі and 21 other words pronounced ''shikan'' (e.g., д»•е®ҳ "government service" and ејӣз·© "relaxation") but not ''shikan'' еҸӘз®Ў.}}) (as practiced by the Tendai sect) that translates the Sanskrit "''{{ill|Еҡamatha|en|Еҡamatha|WD=Q1569252|lt=Еҡamatha|1=Еҡamatha}}'' and ''[[:en:VipaЕӣyanДҒ|VipaЕӣyanДҒ]]''," the two basic forms of {{ill|Buddhist meditation|en|Buddhist meditation|WD=Q1207203|lt=Buddhist meditation|1=Buddhist meditation}}.{{refn|group=note|The term ''shikan'' ({{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}}) is derived from Chinese ''zhЗҗguДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|жӯўи§Җ}}, "[Buddhism] keep mental calm while observing the universe" (cf. the ''[[Mohe Zhiguan]]''){{harvtxt|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267}}), which [[compound (linguistics)|compounds]] ''shi'' or ''zhЗҗ'' ({{lang|zh|жӯў}} ("stop", "stabilize", "''Еӣamatha''") and ''kan'' or ''guДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|и§Җ}}, "observe", "contemplate", "''vipaЕӣyanДҒ''"). An instance of the confusion of {{lang|zh|жӯўиҰі}} for {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} is [[Steve Hagen]]'s claim that "''shi'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|еҸӘ}}'] means tranquility [= '{{lang|zh|жӯў}}'], ''kan'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|з®Ў}}'] means awareness [= '{{lang|zh|иҰі}}'], ''ta'' means hitting exactly the right spot (not one atom off), and ''za'' means to sit."{{harvtxt|Hagen|2007|p=189}}}}

The phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ("just sitting") was used by {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}'s teacher [[:en:Tiantong Rujing|Tiantong Rujing]] (1162вҖ“1228) for silent illumination{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} (Chinese ''mГІzhГ o'' й»ҳз…§; Japanese {{Transliteration|ja|mokushЕҚ}}).{{sfnp|Benson|1989}} According to Koten Benson, in ''mochao'' 
{{quote|The first character, mo, has an element in it that means black or darkness, making the whole character signify "dark, secret, silent, serene, profound" and also "to close the lips, to become silent". The second character, chao, has as element meaning "the brightness of the sun". The whole character translates as "to reflect light, to shine on, to illume or enlighten", as well as "to reflect upon, to look upon, to have insight into". The whole term thus becomes "serene reflection", "silent illumination" or "luminescent darkness".{{sfnp|Benson|1989}}}}

==Practice==
===Classical sources===
"Silent illumination" or "silent reflection" was the hallmark of the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese|1=China}} [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of [[:en:Chinese ChГЎn|Chan]].<ref group="web">Muller, A. Charles, ed.: The {{ill|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|en|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|WD=Q16961807|lt=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|1=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism}}, ed. of 04/03/2008, Chinese Readings Index (Pinyin System) [http://buddhism-dict.net/ddb/indexes/read-zh-py.html]</ref> The first Chan teacher to articulate silent illumination was the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ”1157), who wrote an inscription entitled "silent illumination meditation" (''MokushЕҚ zen'' й»ҳз…§зҰ… or ''MГІzhГ o chГЎn'' й»ҳз…§зҰӘ).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=xii}} Sheng-yen explains that

{{blockquote|In silent illumination, "just sitting" is only the first step. While you maintain the sitting posture, you should also try to establish the "silent" state of the mind. Eventually you reach a point where the mind does not move and yet is very clear. That unmoving mind is "silent," and that clarity of mind is "illumination." This is the meaning of "silent illumination."<ref>Sheng-yen (lecture, 1993), [https://www.ddmbachicago.org/chan/methods/silentillumination/ ''The Silent Illumination Method'']</ref>}}

With the phrase ''shikantaza'' DЕҚgen means "doing only zazen whole-heartedly"{{sfnp|Akishige|1977|p=18}} or "single-minded sitting."{{sfnp|Shaner|1985|p=158}}  According to Merv Fowler, shikantaza is described best as "quiet sitting in open awareness, reflecting directly the reality of life."{{sfnp|Fowler|2005|p=96}} According to Austin, ''shikantaza'' is "an alert condition, performed erect, with no trace of sluggishness or drowsiness."{{sfnp|Austin|1998|p=76}} Fred Reinhard Dallmayr writes, 
{{quote|Regarding practice, Dogen counseled a distinctly nonattached or nonclinging kind of action, that is, an activity completely unconcerned with benefits or the accomplishment of ulterior goals: the activity of 'just sitting' or 'nothing-but-sitting' (''shikantaza'') whereby self-seeking is set aside in a manner resembling a resolute 'dropping off of body and mind.'{{sfnp|Dallmayr|1996|p=178-179}}}}

===Modern sources===
Zen master {{ill|John Daido Loori|en|John Daido Loori|WD=Q6228392|lt=John Daido Loori|1=John Daido Loori}} describes ''shikantaza'' as a challenging practice in spite of its name's simplicity. Mental strength (''joriki'') is not achieved through sustained concentration as in {{ill|Anapanasati|en|Anapanasati|WD=Q2123673|lt=breath meditation|1=Anapanasati}}, but through awareness of the flow of mind, without actively attempting to let go of a thought.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} The user must watch its thoughts, "without analyzing them, judging them, attempting to understand or categorize them," being only aware of them.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} According to him, this helps mental activity move on and produce {{ill|Samadhi|en|Samadhi|WD=Q2217023|lt=Samadhi|1=Samadhi}}.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}}

{{quote|When you're doing ''shikantaza'' you don't try to focus on anything specifically, or to make thoughts go away. You simply allow everything to be just the way it is. Thoughts come, thoughts go, and you simply watch them, you keep your awareness on them. It takes a lot of energy and persistence to sit ''shikantaza'', to not get caught up in daydreaming. But little by little, thoughts begin to slow down, and finally they cease to arise. When the thought disappears, the thinker disappears.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}}}

Commenting on Loori's words, meditation expert Eric Harrison likens ''shikantaza'' to a {{ill|Psychology|en|Psychology|WD=Q9418|lt=psychological|1=Psychology}} process of {{ill|Extinction (psychology)|en|Extinction (psychology)|WD=Q1385098|lt=extinction|1=Extinction (psychology)}}, in which repeated reduction of a behavioral response eventually leads to no response.{{sfnp|Harrison|2017}}

Loori describes awareness as the one thing necessary to the practice of shikantaza.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} This requires a heightened state of mental alertness, which he warns cannot be maintained for too long periods of time. He recommends to practice ''shikantaza'' half an hour to an hour, then stand up and practice ''{{ill|Walking meditation|en|Walking meditation|WD=Q571062|lt=kinhin|1=Walking meditation}}'' in order to relax the mind before sitting down and continuing.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}

{{ill|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|en|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|WD=Q572599|lt=ShunryЕ« Suzuki|1=ShunryЕ« Suzuki}} states about ''shikantaza'', "do not try to stop your mind, but leave everything as it is. Then things will not stay in your mind for so long. Things will come as they come and go as they go. Eventually your clear, empty mind will last fairly long."{{sfnp|Davis|2022|p=293}} For his part, describing the practice's goal as being simply aware of thoughts without getting caught by them, Sean Murphy cites {{ill|Taizan Maezumi|en|Taizan Maezumi|WD=Q1952144|lt=Taizan Maezumi|1=Taizan Maezumi}} as advising to "regard our thoughts as if they were clouds, watching them as they drift from one end of the mind to the other, but making no attempt to hold onto them - and when they pass over the horizon, as they inevitably will, making no attempt to grasp after them.<ref>Sean Murphy, "Guided Meditation: Passing Thoughts", ''{{ill|Yoga Journal|en|Yoga Journal|WD=Q1027421|lt=Yoga Journal|1=Yoga Journal}}'', JanuaryвҖ“February 2003</ref>

Jundo Cohen warns that its meaning of "just sitting" must not be taken too literally, and underlines the importance of awareness.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} When faced against strong emotions or anxious thoughts, Cohen instructs to simply observe them with {{ill|Upekб№ЈДҒ|en|Upekб№ЈДҒ|WD=Q2165710|lt=equanimity|1=Upekб№ЈДҒ}}, "treating them like passing weather clouds". At the same time, he stresses not to play with and being pulled in by thoughts.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} He compares ''shikantaza'' to "the children's puzzle of {{ill|Chinese finger trap|en|Chinese finger trap|WD=Q1384990|lt=Chinese finger cuffs|1=Chinese finger trap}}, which are escaped not by forceful effort, but by non-resistance". Only by dropping the hunt for {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}, accepting everything without grasping or avoiding, can enlightenment be found in it.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}}

====Similar techniques====
A modern technique described as similar to ''shikantaza'' is called "Do Nothing Meditation" by {{ill|Shinzen Young|en|Shinzen Young|WD=Q7497849|lt=Shinzen Young|1=Shinzen Young}}. The user is instructed to let go of all mental intentions, without trying to meditate or concentrate in any way. Any distraction or thought is allowed, unless the user feels they are intentionally thinking or doing something, in whose case they must stop this intention and let it go, including any possible struggle at it. As a result, "eventually the mind feels very spacious, open, and relaxed, but also bright, clear, and vivid".<ref>{{cite web|last=Taft|first=Michael W.|url=https://deconstructingyourself.com/do-nothing-meditation.html|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=September 11, 2017|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Deconstructingyourself.com}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.anahana.com/en/wellbeing-blog/do-nothing-meditation|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=August 25, 2022|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Anahana}}</ref>

Another similar description comes from [[:en:Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj|Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj]], in ''{{ill|I Am That|en|I Am That|WD=Q5976093|lt=I Am That|1=I Am That}}'', where he recommends "letting thoughts flow and watching them and to keep the mind quiet. "The state of freedom from all thoughts will happen suddenly and by the bliss of it you shall recognize it."<ref>Nisargadatta Maharaj, ''I Am That'', p.224-225</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[bg:РЁРёРәР°РҪСӮР°РҙР·Р°]][[de:Shikantaza]][[en:Shikantaza]][[es:Shikantaza]][[et:Shikantaza]][[fa:ШҙЫҢЪ©Ш§ЩҶвҖҢШӘШ§ШІШ§]][[fr:Shikantaza]][[ja:й»ҷз…§зҰ…]][[ko:л¬өмЎ°м„ ]][[pl:Shikantaza]][[pt:Shikantaza]][[sk:Е ikantaza]][[tr:Shikantaza]][[zh:й»ҳз…§зҰӘ]]

{{Short description|Japanese translation of a Chinese term for zazen}}
{{Italic title|reason=}}
{{Zen Buddhism}}
{{nihongo|'''''Shikantaza'''''|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ}} is [[:en:Dogen|Dogen]]'s {{ill|Japanese language|en|Japanese language|WD=Q5287|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese language}} translation of the {{ill|Chinese language|en|Chinese language|WD=Q7850|lt=Chinese|1=Chinese language}} phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}),{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "focus on meditative practice alone", although many modern Western practitioners have interpreted this very differently.{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} The phrase was used by Dogen's teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}}, a monk of the [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of {{ill|Chan Buddhism|en|Chan Buddhism|WD=Q940709|lt=Chan|1=Chan Buddhism}} {{ill|Buddhism|en|Buddhism|WD=Q748|lt=Buddhism|1=Buddhism}}, to refer to the {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=meditation|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}-practice called "silent illumination" ({{zh|s=й»ҳз…§зҰ…}}), or "serene reflection", taught by the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ“1157).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} In Japan, it is associated with the {{ill|Zen|en|Zen|WD=Q7953|lt=Zen|1=Zen}} [[:en:Soto school|Soto school]], Dogen's offshoot of Caodong. Some practitioners teach that ''shikantaza'' means that one should not focus attention on a specific object (such as the breath), instead "just sitting" in a state of conscious awareness; however, the 13th-century origin of the expression indicates a general emphasis on meditation in any form as sufficient for spiritual enlightenment. The original teaching was meant to criticize the complicated ceremony, abstruse study, endless tracing of spiritual lineage, and other aspects of Buddhism that even by the 12th century had been identified as excessive.

==Etymology and meaning==
The term ''shikantaza'' is the [[Kanji#On'yomi (Sino-Japanese reading)|Sino-Japanese reading]]{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} of ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' (еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ){{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}}<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just sitting",{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}}{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} "nothing but sitting",{{sfnp|Fischer-Schreiber|Schuhmacher|Woerner|1989|p=321}} "meditation of just sitting",<ref group=web name="Muller"/> "just mind [yourself] sitting".{{sfnp|Shengyen|2008|p=94}} ''ZhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ўжү“еқҗ / зҘҮз®Ўжү“еқҗ}}) translates as follows:
* ''zhЗҗguЗҺn'' ({{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}}, J. ''shikan''; or {{lang|zh|зҘҮз®Ў}}, with {{lang|zh|зҘҮ}} [zhЗҗ] serving as a variant of {{lang|zh|еҸӘ}} [zhЗҗ]<ref group=web name="Muller"/>), "by all means; merely, simply; only concerned with",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to focus exclusively on";<ref group=web name="Muller"/>
* ''dЗҺzuГІ'' {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}}, "[Buddhism/Daoism] sit in meditation",{{sfnp|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267, 182}} "to {{ill|Squatting position|en|Squatting position|WD=Q2540134|lt=squat|1=Squatting position}}, sit down cross-legged", which corresponds with {{ill|Sanskrit|en|Sanskrit|WD=Q11059|lt=Sanskrit|1=Sanskrit}} ''utkuб№ӯuka-stha'';<ref group=web name="Muller"/>

The inspiration for this teaching derives from a pivotal episode reportedly occurring sometime in the early 1220s (Song dynasty), at {{ill|Tiantong Temple|en|Tiantong Temple|WD=Q10940602|lt=Tiantong Mountain Monastery|1=Tiantong Temple}} ({{lang|zh|еӨ©з«ҘеҜә}}, also known as Jingde Monastery {{lang|zh|жҷҜеҫ·еҜә}}, east of modern-day {{ill|Ningbo|en|Ningbo|WD=Q42780|lt=Ningbo|1=Ningbo}}). An exchange took place between the eminent Chinese {{ill|Caodong school|en|Caodong school|WD=Q1964867|lt=Caodong|1=Caodong school}} teacher {{ill|Rujing|en|Rujing|WD=Q5964809|lt=Rujing|1=Rujing}} and his disciples. In particular, it focuses on an inspiration by one of Rujing's Japanese disciples, {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}, who would later found the [[SЕҚtЕҚ|SЕҚtЕҚ Zen]] sect:

{{Blockquote|text=Then, one day during late night seated meditation, Reverend Jing entered the hall and admonished the great assembly for sleeping, saying:

"Inquiring into Zen is the sloughing off of body and mind [{{lang|zh|иә«еҝғи„ұиҗҪ}}].
''There is no need for burning incense, making prostrations, recollecting buddhas, practicing repentances, or reading sЕ«tras.'' Just sit [in meditation] [{{lang|zh|зҘҮз®ЎгҒ«жү“еқҗ}}]; only then will you attain it."

At that time, hearing this, the Master [DЕҚgen] immediately had a great awakening... .<ref>{{cite book |last1=Foulk |first1=T. Griffith |title=DenkЕҚroku (Record of the Transmission) of Illumination by the Great Ancestor, Zen Master Keizan, Vol. II |date=2017вҖ“2018 |publisher=SЕҚtЕҚshЕ« ShЕ«muchЕҚ |page=535-536 |url=https://www.sotozen.com/eng/library/denkoroku/index.html |access-date=28 July 2024 |chapter=51}}</ref> [emphasis added]}}

While T.G. Foulk's translation here reads only "sit", he and other interpreters clarify that the meaning of {{lang|zh|жү“еқҗ}} is generally broad, meaning more than simply sitting.{{sfnp|Buswell Jr.|Lopez Jr.|2014|p=Entry: shikan taza}} The original exchange between Rujing and his disciples indicates a clear meaning of the teaching: that high-flung ceremony and study are unnecessary and irrelevant, that {{ill|Zazen|en|Zazen|WD=Q167894|lt=Zazen|1=Zazen}}, {{ill|Dhyana in Buddhism|en|Dhyana in Buddhism|WD=Q6577829|lt=dhyana|1=Dhyana in Buddhism}}, and similar meditation practice of whatever kind (whether sitting, resting, breathing, gazing at a scene, walking, or simply engaging in silence) should be sufficiently effective.

{{ill|James Ishmael Ford|en|James Ishmael Ford|WD=Q6136610|lt=James Ishmael Ford|1=James Ishmael Ford}} states that "some trace the root of this word [''shikantaza''] to the Japanese pronunciation of Sanskrit [[:en:Vipassana|Vipassana]], though this is far from certain."{{sfnp|Ford|2006|p=29-30}} This etymological error about {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} (''shikan'', "only", "just") is rooted in the fact that Japanese has many [[:en:homophones|homophones]] pronounced ''shikan''. It stems from a more commonly used Japanese word, namely {{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}} (''shikan'', "concentration and observation"{{refn|group=note|name=TSS|Watanabe ToshirЕҚ (жёЎйӮҠж•ҸйғҺ), Edmund R. Skrzypczak, and Paul Snowden, eds. (2003), ''Kenkyusha's New Japanese-English Dictionary'' (ж–°е’ҢиӢұеӨ§иҫһе…ё), 5th edition, Kenkyusha, 1125. This bilingual dictionary lists жӯўиҰі and 21 other words pronounced ''shikan'' (e.g., д»•е®ҳ "government service" and ејӣз·© "relaxation") but not ''shikan'' еҸӘз®Ў.}}) (as practiced by the Tendai sect) that translates the Sanskrit "''{{ill|Еҡamatha|en|Еҡamatha|WD=Q1569252|lt=Еҡamatha|1=Еҡamatha}}'' and ''[[:en:VipaЕӣyanДҒ|VipaЕӣyanДҒ]]''," the two basic forms of {{ill|Buddhist meditation|en|Buddhist meditation|WD=Q1207203|lt=Buddhist meditation|1=Buddhist meditation}}.{{refn|group=note|The term ''shikan'' ({{lang|ja|жӯўиҰі}}) is derived from Chinese ''zhЗҗguДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|жӯўи§Җ}}, "[Buddhism] keep mental calm while observing the universe" (cf. the ''[[Mohe Zhiguan]]''){{harvtxt|DeFrancis|2003|p=1267}}), which [[compound (linguistics)|compounds]] ''shi'' or ''zhЗҗ'' ({{lang|zh|жӯў}} ("stop", "stabilize", "''Еӣamatha''") and ''kan'' or ''guДҒn'' ({{lang|zh|и§Җ}}, "observe", "contemplate", "''vipaЕӣyanДҒ''"). An instance of the confusion of {{lang|zh|жӯўиҰі}} for {{lang|zh|еҸӘз®Ў}} is [[Steve Hagen]]'s claim that "''shi'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|еҸӘ}}'] means tranquility [= '{{lang|zh|жӯў}}'], ''kan'' [Hagen is referring to DЕҚgen's '{{lang|zh|з®Ў}}'] means awareness [= '{{lang|zh|иҰі}}'], ''ta'' means hitting exactly the right spot (not one atom off), and ''za'' means to sit."{{harvtxt|Hagen|2007|p=189}}}}

The phrase ''zhЗҗguЗҺn dЗҺzuГІ'' ("just sitting") was used by {{ill|DЕҚgen|en|DЕҚgen|WD=Q318064|lt=DЕҚgen|1=DЕҚgen}}'s teacher [[:en:Tiantong Rujing|Tiantong Rujing]] (1162вҖ“1228) for silent illumination{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=17}} (Chinese ''mГІzhГ o'' й»ҳз…§; Japanese {{Transliteration|ja|mokushЕҚ}}).{{sfnp|Benson|1989}} According to Koten Benson, in ''mochao'' 
{{quote|The first character, mo, has an element in it that means black or darkness, making the whole character signify "dark, secret, silent, serene, profound" and also "to close the lips, to become silent". The second character, chao, has as element meaning "the brightness of the sun". The whole character translates as "to reflect light, to shine on, to illume or enlighten", as well as "to reflect upon, to look upon, to have insight into". The whole term thus becomes "serene reflection", "silent illumination" or "luminescent darkness".{{sfnp|Benson|1989}}}}

==Practice==
===Classical sources===
"Silent illumination" or "silent reflection" was the hallmark of the {{ill|China|en|China|WD=Q148|lt=Chinese|1=China}} [[:en:Caodong|Caodong]] school of [[:en:Chinese ChГЎn|Chan]].<ref group="web">Muller, A. Charles, ed.: The {{ill|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|en|Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|WD=Q16961807|lt=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism|1=Digital Dictionary of Buddhism}}, ed. of 04/03/2008, Chinese Readings Index (Pinyin System) [http://buddhism-dict.net/ddb/indexes/read-zh-py.html]</ref> The first Chan teacher to articulate silent illumination was the Caodong master {{ill|Hongzhi Zhengjue|en|Hongzhi Zhengjue|WD=Q1060944|lt=Hongzhi Zhengjue|1=Hongzhi Zhengjue}} (1091вҖ”1157), who wrote an inscription entitled "silent illumination meditation" (''MokushЕҚ zen'' й»ҳз…§зҰ… or ''MГІzhГ o chГЎn'' й»ҳз…§зҰӘ).{{sfnp|Leighton|2000|p=xii}} Sheng-yen explains that

{{blockquote|In silent illumination, "just sitting" is only the first step. While you maintain the sitting posture, you should also try to establish the "silent" state of the mind. Eventually you reach a point where the mind does not move and yet is very clear. That unmoving mind is "silent," and that clarity of mind is "illumination." This is the meaning of "silent illumination."<ref>Sheng-yen (lecture, 1993), [https://www.ddmbachicago.org/chan/methods/silentillumination/ ''The Silent Illumination Method'']</ref>}}

With the phrase ''shikantaza'' DЕҚgen means "doing only zazen whole-heartedly"{{sfnp|Akishige|1977|p=18}} or "single-minded sitting."{{sfnp|Shaner|1985|p=158}}  According to Merv Fowler, shikantaza is described best as "quiet sitting in open awareness, reflecting directly the reality of life."{{sfnp|Fowler|2005|p=96}} According to Austin, ''shikantaza'' is "an alert condition, performed erect, with no trace of sluggishness or drowsiness."{{sfnp|Austin|1998|p=76}} Fred Reinhard Dallmayr writes, 
{{quote|Regarding practice, Dogen counseled a distinctly nonattached or nonclinging kind of action, that is, an activity completely unconcerned with benefits or the accomplishment of ulterior goals: the activity of 'just sitting' or 'nothing-but-sitting' (''shikantaza'') whereby self-seeking is set aside in a manner resembling a resolute 'dropping off of body and mind.'{{sfnp|Dallmayr|1996|p=178-179}}}}

===Modern sources===
Zen master {{ill|John Daido Loori|en|John Daido Loori|WD=Q6228392|lt=John Daido Loori|1=John Daido Loori}} describes ''shikantaza'' as a challenging practice in spite of its name's simplicity. Mental strength (''joriki'') is not achieved through sustained concentration as in {{ill|Anapanasati|en|Anapanasati|WD=Q2123673|lt=breath meditation|1=Anapanasati}}, but through awareness of the flow of mind, without actively attempting to let go of a thought.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} The user must watch its thoughts, "without analyzing them, judging them, attempting to understand or categorize them," being only aware of them.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} According to him, this helps mental activity move on and produce {{ill|Samadhi|en|Samadhi|WD=Q2217023|lt=Samadhi|1=Samadhi}}.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}}

{{quote|When you're doing ''shikantaza'' you don't try to focus on anything specifically, or to make thoughts go away. You simply allow everything to be just the way it is. Thoughts come, thoughts go, and you simply watch them, you keep your awareness on them. It takes a lot of energy and persistence to sit ''shikantaza'', to not get caught up in daydreaming. But little by little, thoughts begin to slow down, and finally they cease to arise. When the thought disappears, the thinker disappears.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}}}

Commenting on Loori's words, meditation expert Eric Harrison likens ''shikantaza'' to a {{ill|Psychology|en|Psychology|WD=Q9418|lt=psychological|1=Psychology}} process of {{ill|Extinction (psychology)|en|Extinction (psychology)|WD=Q1385098|lt=extinction|1=Extinction (psychology)}}, in which repeated reduction of a behavioral response eventually leads to no response.{{sfnp|Harrison|2017}}

Loori describes awareness as the one thing necessary to the practice of shikantaza.{{sfnp|Loori|2002}} This requires a heightened state of mental alertness, which he warns cannot be maintained for too long periods of time. He recommends to practice ''shikantaza'' half an hour to an hour, then stand up and practice ''{{ill|Walking meditation|en|Walking meditation|WD=Q571062|lt=kinhin|1=Walking meditation}}'' in order to relax the mind before sitting down and continuing.{{sfnp|Loori|2005}}

{{ill|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|en|ShunryЕ« Suzuki|WD=Q572599|lt=ShunryЕ« Suzuki|1=ShunryЕ« Suzuki}} states about ''shikantaza'', "do not try to stop your mind, but leave everything as it is. Then things will not stay in your mind for so long. Things will come as they come and go as they go. Eventually your clear, empty mind will last fairly long."{{sfnp|Davis|2022|p=293}} For his part, describing the practice's goal as being simply aware of thoughts without getting caught by them, Sean Murphy cites {{ill|Taizan Maezumi|en|Taizan Maezumi|WD=Q1952144|lt=Taizan Maezumi|1=Taizan Maezumi}} as advising to "regard our thoughts as if they were clouds, watching them as they drift from one end of the mind to the other, but making no attempt to hold onto them - and when they pass over the horizon, as they inevitably will, making no attempt to grasp after them.<ref>Sean Murphy, "Guided Meditation: Passing Thoughts", ''{{ill|Yoga Journal|en|Yoga Journal|WD=Q1027421|lt=Yoga Journal|1=Yoga Journal}}'', JanuaryвҖ“February 2003</ref>

Jundo Cohen warns that its meaning of "just sitting" must not be taken too literally, and underlines the importance of awareness.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} When faced against strong emotions or anxious thoughts, Cohen instructs to simply observe them with {{ill|Upekб№ЈДҒ|en|Upekб№ЈДҒ|WD=Q2165710|lt=equanimity|1=Upekб№ЈДҒ}}, "treating them like passing weather clouds". At the same time, he stresses not to play with and being pulled in by thoughts.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}} He compares ''shikantaza'' to "the children's puzzle of {{ill|Chinese finger trap|en|Chinese finger trap|WD=Q1384990|lt=Chinese finger cuffs|1=Chinese finger trap}}, which are escaped not by forceful effort, but by non-resistance". Only by dropping the hunt for {{ill|Enlightenment in Buddhism|en|Enlightenment in Buddhism|WD=Q1137019|lt=enlightenment|1=Enlightenment in Buddhism}}, accepting everything without grasping or avoiding, can enlightenment be found in it.{{sfnp|Cohen|2020}}

====Similar techniques====
A modern technique described as similar to ''shikantaza'' is called "Do Nothing Meditation" by {{ill|Shinzen Young|en|Shinzen Young|WD=Q7497849|lt=Shinzen Young|1=Shinzen Young}}. The user is instructed to let go of all mental intentions, without trying to meditate or concentrate in any way. Any distraction or thought is allowed, unless the user feels they are intentionally thinking or doing something, in whose case they must stop this intention and let it go, including any possible struggle at it. As a result, "eventually the mind feels very spacious, open, and relaxed, but also bright, clear, and vivid".<ref>{{cite web|last=Taft|first=Michael W.|url=https://deconstructingyourself.com/do-nothing-meditation.html|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=September 11, 2017|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Deconstructingyourself.com}}</ref><ref>{{cite web|url=https://www.anahana.com/en/wellbeing-blog/do-nothing-meditation|title=Do Nothing Meditation|date=August 25, 2022|accessdate=May 1, 2023|publisher=Anahana}}</ref>

Another similar description comes from [[:en:Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj|Sri Nisargadatta Maharaj]], in ''{{ill|I Am That|en|I Am That|WD=Q5976093|lt=I Am That|1=I Am That}}'', where he recommends "letting thoughts flow and watching them and to keep the mind quiet. "The state of freedom from all thoughts will happen suddenly and by the bliss of it you shall recognize it."<ref>Nisargadatta Maharaj, ''I Am That'', p.224-225</ref>
